summary: Matched the Dolphin OS reset-switch module by replacing large inline assembly bodies in src/dolphin/os/OSResetSW.c with C implementations, moving former sbss data from asm/dolphin/os/OSResetSW_data.s into static C globals, and updating related OS time/reset-switch declarations. The PR notes the implementation came "From prime decomp". The change removed the separate OSResetSW_data assembly object from obj_files.mk and added time conversion/API support needed by the C rewrite.
what_changed: Replaced inline assembly for __OSResetSWInterruptHandler, OSGetResetButtonState, and OSGetResetSwitchState in src/dolphin/os/OSResetSW.c with C code.
Deleted asm/dolphin/os/OSResetSW_data.s, which had defined sbss symbols ResetCallback, Down, OSResetSW_LastState, HoldUp, and HoldDown.
Recreated the deleted reset-switch sbss data as static C globals: ResetCallback, Down, LastState, HoldUp, and HoldDown.
Changed OSGetResetSwitchState declaration from s32 OSGetResetSwitchState(OSResetSW_Unk1 *) to BOOL OSGetResetSwitchState(void).
Added __OSGetSystemTime(void) declaration to include/dolphin/os/OSTime.h and removed the local extern declaration from src/dolphin/os/OS.c.
Added OSMicrosecondsToTicks(usec) macro to OSTime.h and used it for the reset-switch 100 microsecond timing checks.
Removed OSResetSW_data.s.o from obj_files.mk because the reset-switch data was no longer assembled separately.
The new C code models reset-switch state using __PIRegs[0], __OSMaskInterrupts, OSDisableInterrupts/OSRestoreInterrupts, __OSStartTime, GameChoice, and OS tick conversion macros.
smart_moves: Migrated anonymous assembly sbss symbols into typed static C globals, reducing reliance on separate data assembly while preserving module-local state.
Introduced a proper OSTime header declaration for __OSGetSystemTime instead of duplicating an untyped extern in OS.c and OSResetSW.c.

Used OSTime arithmetic and named conversion macros such as OSMicrosecondsToTicks, OSMillisecondsToTicks, and OSSecondsToTicks to express timing constants that were previously encoded as multiply/shift assembly sequences.
Captured ResetCallback into a local callback variable before clearing the global and invoking it, matching the assembly behavior while avoiding repeated global access.
Kept PI reset-switch register access explicit through __PIRegs[0] and the observed 0x00010000 bit test, making hardware-state logic readable without hiding the matching-sensitive details.
decomp_lessons: When matching OS code that stores sbss state in a companion *_data.s file, try replacing those labels with static C globals of the narrowest known type once the owning C translation unit is matched.

Header cleanup is part of matching: once a function such as __OSGetSystemTime is used by multiple OS files, declare it in the appropriate public/internal OS header rather than relying on local unk_t externs.

Assembly constants for time windows can often be recovered as OS tick conversion macros; in this PR, the 100 microsecond and 40 millisecond reset-switch windows became OSMicrosecondsToTicks(100) and OSMillisecondsToTicks(40).

Function signatures should be corrected as confidence improves; OSGetResetSwitchState became a no-argument BOOL-returning wrapper around OSGetResetButtonState, replacing an earlier s32 signature with an unknown pointer parameter.
After migrating assembly data into C, update obj_files.mk to remove the obsolete assembly object or the build may still try to link stale split data.
Volatile hardware register arrays can be declared with fixed addresses, as shown by extern vu32 __PIRegs[12] : 0xCC003000, when matching low-level Dolphin OS hardware code.
naming_conventions: Use Dolphin OS-style names for reset switch routines: __OSResetSWInterruptHandler, OSGetResetButtonState, OSGetResetSwitchState.
Prefer typed BOOL state names for reset-switch state variables: Down and LastState rather than generic unk_t labels.
Use OSTime for 64-bit time state such as HoldUp, HoldDown, and __OSStartTime.
Preserve hardware/global names when evidence exists: GameChoice at 0x800030E3 and __PIRegs at 0xCC003000.
Use OS tick conversion macro names in the existing Dolphin style: OSTicksToSeconds, OSSecondsToTicks, OSMillisecondsToTicks, OSMicrosecondsToTicks, OSNanosecondsToTicks.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Converted inline assembly control flow into structured C while preserving interrupt disable/restore boundaries around OSGetResetButtonState.
Represented the interrupt handler's busy-wait loop as a C while loop checking both elapsed system time and the PI reset-switch bit.
Matched assembly time calculations by adding/using conversion macros instead of manually reproducing magic multiply constants.
Preserved one-shot callback semantics by masking OS_INTR_PI_RSW, checking ResetCallback for NULL, clearing it, and then calling through the saved function pointer.
Replaced the tiny assembly wrapper OSGetResetSwitchState with a C wrapper returning OSGetResetButtonState().
Deleted the separate data assembly file after all its symbols were owned by the C translation unit.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.comment: GitHub doesn't say this was deleted, is it an empty file now?
review_feedback.resolution_or_context: The diff excerpt shows the file was deleted; the author replied "Huh, weird". No further technical review feedback is present in the provided slice.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs touching OSResetSW.c to verify whether LastState or ResetCallback naming changed after this match.
Search for additional OSTime.h changes around __OSGetSystemTime and OSMicrosecondsToTicks to confirm final header conventions.
Check whether other *_data.s files with only sbss labels were later migrated into static C globals using the same pattern.
Look for follow-up review or commits clarifying why GitHub displayed the OSResetSW_data.s deletion oddly.
